column8: This service reads inputs on annual CM-929 and CM-908 forms received. It does not serve as a principal basis for decisions or actions with significant effect on an individual or entity's civil rights, civil liberties, or privacy, access to critical government resources or services, or human health and safety. Any form with unreadable inputs or responses that could result in a change in benefits are directed to a human reviewer for final review and action.
column11: The AI is intended to allow the claims examiner more time to focus on more critical adjudication tasks.  The CM-929 and CM-908 forms amount to tens of thousands of incoming correspondence on an annual basis.  Claims examiners were cumulatively spending an excess amount of time each year reviewing the forms only for indications of a change in circumstances (based on a check-the-box indicator) or similar notice.  This would then be followed by the entry of a code signifying receipt in the Automated Support Package.  Only a very small number of forms received each year require a modification of benefits.  AI is easily able to handle this task, and ensures that forms indicating a change in circumstances are reviewed by a human, while the remainder are processed by the AI, reducing wait times in other areas of the adjudication process for claimants.

column11: The input is state-submitted response files that include occupation title and sometimes job description of the surveyed units. The autocoder reads the job title and assigns up to two 6-digit Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) codes along with their probabilities as recommendations for human coders. Codes above a certain threshold are appended to the submitted response file and sent back to states to assist them with their SOC code assignment.
column12: Improving data accuracy and saving time for SOC coders

column11: BLS receives bulk data from some corporations related to the cost of goods they sell and services they provide. Consumer Price Index (CPI) staff have hand-coded a segment of the items in these data into Entry Level Item (ELI) codes. To accept and make use of these bulk data transfers at scale, BLS has begun to use machine learning to label data with ELI codes. The machine learning model takes as input word frequency counts from item descriptions. Logistic regression is then used to estimate the probability of each item being classified in each ELI category based on the word frequency categorizations. The highest probability category is selected for inclusion in the data. Any selected classifications that do not meet a certain probability threshold are flagged for human review. Benefits: real-time turnaround, productivity improvements, cost savings.
column12: Efficiency of item classification efforts

column8: Used for survey processing for statistical purposes
column11: The BLS productivity office publishes measures of hours worked by major sector and industry. As part of the calculation for this measure, BLS uses Current Employment Statistics (CES) data on hours paid is used to estimate hours for payroll workers. Additional adjustments are made to this measure, such as removing paid time off (PTO), adding off-the-clock (OTC) hours, and adding in hours worked by self-employed and unpaid family workers. BLS uses the Current Population Survey (CPS) dataset to identify the amount of hours that are worked off-the-clock (OTC) by workers. There are some workers in the CPS dataset that do not report whether their time off was paid, or whether they get paid hourly or not. This data needs to be imputed to calculate the ratio of total hours worked to paid hours worked. A random forest model is used to predict the responses for workers that did not report this information by training it on characteristics (such as industry, occupation, education level, age, etc.) of respondents that have reported that information.

column8: Used for survey processing for statistical purposes
column11:
BLS establishment-based surveys, such as the Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII), the National Compensation Survey (NCS), and the Occupational Requirements Survey (ORS), use the Longitudinal Database (LDB) as the frame for their survey samples. For each of these programs, there is a significant time-lag between when the sample is drawn for a program and when data collection begins. A critical and time-consuming part of data collection is sample refinement: checking the latest Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) and LDB data to adjust for any changes to the sampled establishment during that time-lag. The QCEW/LDB Frame API allows fast retrieval of the latest QCEW/LDB data for a nationwide sample so that users can perform sample refinement more efficiently. The SOII program uses the Frame API to perform over 20 sample refinement comparison checks, which outputs a report for the data collectors.

The Frame API uses TF-IDF vectors and cosine similarity to compare company names, mailing addresses, and unit descriptions between the survey sample and the latest QCEW/LDB data to determine what components may have changed. The Frame API also has the flexibility for the users to match records using other combinations of variables.
column12:
Efficiency and data quality 
improvements

column8: AI headset provides voice-accessed information, checklists, regulations, and multimedia capture assistance only; inspectors retain full authority to determine violations and enforcement actions. Does not meet high-impact definition per M-25-21 Section 5."
column11: Inspection: An AI headset offers a hands-free, voice-controlled solution that enhances the efficiency, safety, and accuracy of MSHA inspectors using the Inspection Application System (IAS). By enabling inspectors to access inspection checklists, capture photos and videos, and input data through voice commands, the headset reduces reliance on manual data entry and handheld devices in mining environments. It also supports real-time communication with field office supervisors and data collection, streamlining the inspection process and improving documentation. When integrated with IAS or compatible platforms, an AI headset  can significantly modernize field inspections, ensuring better compliance and faster reporting.
column12: Improve Inspection Onsite Documentation Time: The intended purpose of the AI headset is to provide a hands-free, voice-activated wearable solution that enhances the productivity, safety, and effectiveness of frontline government workers, such as MSHA inspectors. The expected benefits include significant cost savings through reduced inspection times and fewer repeat site visits, as well as improved documentation accuracy through real-time photo, video, and voice note capture. The device also enables live communication with remote experts, accelerating decision-making and reducing downtime.
column13: Outputs: For MSHA inspectors using the Inspection Application System (IAS), the AI system outputs of the AI headset are designed to streamline field operations, enhance safety, and improve data accuracy. These outputs are delivered through a combination of visual, audio, and data-driven interfaces that support real-time decision-making and documentation. The primary output is a high-resolution micro-display that appears in the inspector’s line of sight, showing inspection forms and data entry screens, mine data, safety protocols, or previous violation histories pulled from IAS or the Mine Data Retrieval System (MDRS)
